items.title: Sitting for more than 30 minutes at a time linked to higher risk of cancer death
items.content_html:
Study suggests even light activity such as ironing could reduce health risks linked to prolonged sedentary behaviour

Sitting for longer than half an hour at a time each day raises the risk of dying from cancer, a study suggests.

Researchers who tracked more than 90,000 people over a decade found that sitting or lying down while awake for more than 30 minutes in one period each day was associated with an increased risk of cancer death. The risk increases for every additional hour of continuous inactivity, the findings suggest.

items.title: At least 21 dead as Russia launches massive drone and missile attack on Kyiv
items.content_html:
More than 70 missiles fired at Ukraine capital as Russia faces fuel shortages after strikes against its oil refineries

At least 21 people were killed and dozens injured overnight in Kyiv, local authorities said, in what the city’s mayor, Vitali Klitschko, called the worst Russian attack on the capital during more than four years of air assault on Ukraine.

Russia used nearly 500 drones and more than 70 missiles in the hours-long attack on Kyiv and other parts of the country in the early hours of Thursday. Loud explosions shook the capital for several hours as waves of drones as well as cruise and ballistic missiles came towards it and Ukraine’s air defence attempted to shoot them down.

Les Lions de la Téranga menaient 2 buts à 0 face aux Belges, avant de se faire remonter aux 86e et 89e minutes. Un pénalty dans les ultimes minutes des prolongations clôture le match et ainsi les espoirs sénégalais de remporter la Coupe du monde. La rancœur a poussé certains joueurs et membres de l’équipe technique à prendre position. Précisions d'Elimane Ndao, correspondant de France 24 à Dakar.

items.content_html: The Vatican excommunicated six bishops from the ultraconservative Society of St Pius X , while warning that any lay believers who "formally adhere" to the breakaway Catholic group would suffer the same fate. items.title: Young Indonesian couple publicly caned after kissing on TikTok

Unmarried man and woman whipped 21 times each because they had violated province’s version of Islamic law

A young couple in Indonesia’s conservative Aceh province have been publicly caned after a Sharia court convicted them of violating Islamic law by kissing during a TikTok livestream.

The court ordered the couple, a 22-year-old man and a 25-year-old woman, to be whipped with a rattan cane 21 times each for kissing without being married. At least 100 people witnessed the caning, carried out by a group of people wearing robes and hoods on a stage in Bustanussalatin City Park in Banda Aceh.

items.title: Understanding Ebola’s wildlife origins is crucial to preventing next big outbreak
items.content_html:
If we don’t know the source, not only do humans remain at risk but wildlife can suffer needlessly via retaliation

While virologists and public health departments were palpitating over the news of an Andes virus infectious disease outbreak on a cruise ship (13 cases, three deaths), in the Democratic Republic of the Congo the Bundibugyo virus, the root of the current Ebola outbreak (currently more than 1,250 cases and at least 362 deaths), was smouldering under the radar.

Bundibugyo virus is a horrifying, highly fatal pathogen. Symptom onset is sudden and includes headaches, diarrhoea, malfunctioning kidneys and liver, and, less frequently, internal and external bleeding (hence the term “haemorrhagic disease”). Grimly, contagiousness remains after death, meaning the family and loved ones of the deceased can be exposed when they wash and clothe the body in preparation for the funeral.

items.title: China’s ethnic unity law denounced as ‘forced assimilation’ by rights groups
items.content_html:
Law comes into effect that critics fear will further erode rights of Uyghurs and Tibetans, as well as allow Beijing to pursue dissidents abroad

A new ethnic unity law has come into effect in China despite warnings from Taiwan, the United Nations and rights groups that it could threaten freedoms, especially for minorities.

The Law on Promoting Ethnic Unity and Progress aims to forge a “shared” national identity among ethnic groups, for example by strengthening the status of Mandarin as the official language. But overseas campaigners have argued it will further degrade the rights of ethnic minorities, such as Uyghurs and Tibetans, that Beijing is accused of persecuting.

items.title: Nos parents ont menti : 10 ans après le Brexit
items.content_html:
Si le gouvernement de Margaret Thatcher était le plus long du 20ème siècle au Royaume-Uni, son agonie allait être la plus brutale. On peut la limiter aux les 18 minutes de discours parlementaire avec lequel, le 13 novembre 1990, son vice-premier ministre, Sir Geoffrey Howe, fidèle dès la première heure, il a retiré sa loyauté. C'est un morceau d'oratoire important, qui a marqué l'histoire. Connu pour sa rhétorique plutôt somnifère, la sobriété de Howe a contribué à lui donner de l'assurance à cette occasion, et son intervention, aussi chargée de données que de poids moral, ne l'a pas été. va seulement démanteler le gouvernement de l'époque, mais a réussi à perdurer comme une prophétie : encore en 2026, nous pouvons méditer sur ce que Howe a dit en 1990. Évoquant les tentatives du premier Harold Macmillan, au début des années soixante, pour rejoindre le projet européen, Howe a mis en garde contre la tentation de « se replier dans un ghetto de sentimentalité sur notre propre passé », et a exhorté le Royaume-Uni à ne pas se dissocier « des réalités »."Le risque" pour le Royaume-Uni ne résidait pas tant dans "les impositions" de Bruxelles que "dans l'isolement".Et il a prévenu : « Les pays européens peuvent avancer sans nous.»

items.title: Donald Trump threatens to annihilate Iran after crossfire over Hormuz – as it happened
items.content_html:
Iran attacked Bahrain and Kuwait after US strikes, and threatened a ‘complete halt’ to talks

  • US and Iran trade strikes as both sides accuse the other of endangering ceasefire

We will soon be closing this liveblog, but you’ll be able to stay up-to-date with our ongoing coverage of the Middle East here.

Here is a summary of today’s events:

Iran launched drone and missile attacks Sunday targeting Bahrain and Kuwait in response to US airstrikes that hit the Islamic Republic, and threatened a “complete halt” in negotiations to end the war if Washington continues its attacks.

US president Donald Trump accused Iran of violating the ceasefire agreement in a post of social media and said the US may be “forced to militarily complete the job”. Iran also accused the US of violating the ceasefire agreement.

JD Vance continued to reiterate the administration’s triumphant line on the war with Iran hours before the latest round of strikes were exchanged. “America wins either way,” he said.

Iran’s foreign minister Abbas Araghchi was in Baghdad for a meeting with his Iraqi counterpart. He called for a security framework to be established with the Gulf nations after it struck US bases in Kuwait and Bahrain in retaliation to US strikes.

The Iranian Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps IRCG has said on state-run SNN TV that it will respond with more force if there are any more blow-for-blow attacks from the US.

Countries including Jordan, the UAE and Italy all condemned Iran’s attacks.

items.content_html: A proposed US-Iran deal to reopen the Strait of Hormuz is raising hopes for global shipping and oil markets. But naval mines, high insurance costs and geopolitical risks mean disruption could persist for months.

items.title: US-Iran deal: When will oil prices fall?
items.content_html: A potential US-Iran deal to end the war and reopen the Strait of Hormuz could ease the global energy crunch, but oil prices and supplies may take months to stabilize as shipping restarts and infrastructure recovers.

items.title: Mines in the Strait of Hormuz: How dangerous are they?
items.content_html: There are still mines in the Straight of Hormuz, delaying the opening for commercial shipping. How dangerous are naval mines, and what can be done to clear them?

items.title: Swiss to vote on whether to cap population at 10 million
items.content_html: On June 14, Swiss voters will be able to decide on a far-right initiative to curb future immigration. What will it mean for the economy — and the country's European neighbors — if they approve the proposal?

items.title: Elon Musk becomes world's first trillionaire after SpaceX IPO
items.content_html: SpaceX floated on the Nasdaq stock exchange on Friday, with shares trading 25% above the opening price at one point. The IPO has made Musk the world's first trillionaire, at least on paper.

items.title: British artist David Hockney dies at 88
items.content_html: Considered a superstar of pop art, the UK painter was one of the world's most influential artists during his lifetime.

items.title: Lab-grown Tyrannosaurus leather bag fails to sell at auction
items.content_html: The handbag promoted as the "world's first T. rex leather product" didn't find a buyer at auction. Is it more chicken than dinosaur?

items.title: Preaching and teaching in a warming world
items.content_html: US pastors, a Ugandan musician and salmon-friendly tech delivered good environmental news this week.

items.title: Is there anything GLP-1 agonist drugs cannot do?
items.content_html: Every other month, researchers find new uses for GLP-1 agonist drugs, like Mounjaro and Ozempic — now even as a treatment for substance abuse. It's classic drug repurposing, a technique that saved lives during COVID.

items.title: Musk's SpaceX IPO holds both promise and peril
items.content_html: SpaceX, Elon Musk's rocket and AI company, presents potential for investors. But its futuristic, unproven plans also carry significant financial risks.

items.title: Sex doll art sparks debate at German president's residence
items.content_html: Beyond a headline-grabbing bronze, contemporary artworks draw attention to political power at an exhibition held at Bellevue Palace before the state building closes for renovation.

items.title: 'Disclosure Day': For Spielberg, aliens are not just fiction
items.content_html: As Steven Spielberg releases a new film on extraterrestrials, here's a brief history of modern alien conspiracies and why scientists are now taking UFO reports seriously.

items.title: The climate conversation many US pastors avoid
items.content_html: As the Trump administration undoes scores of environmental protections, some Christian leaders are inviting their congregations to do as the Bible asks and be good stewards of the land. But many others stay silent.

items.title: Leipzig Bachfest 2026: Bach's music as hit parade
items.content_html: The Leipzig Bach Festival honors the composer with a hit parade. Over 8,000 fans from 20 countries cast their votes. But Bach is celebrated in many other countries around the world as well.

items.title: Why Anthropic has the edge over OpenAI in IPO race
items.content_html: The rivalry between Sam Altman and Dario Amodei is entering a new phase. Both AI bosses are planning public listings and hope to use the money to outpace each other in the race to build superintelligent AI.

items.title: Depression in romantic relationships: You, me and the illness
items.content_html: Depression can be a huge challenge for a relationship. Loved ones of someone with depression often feel helpless. But there is actually a lot they can do ― and a lot they shouldn't.

items.title: Jafar Panahi's prison sentence confirmed by Iranian court
items.content_html: Following a retrial for a verdict that had been reached while the prominent Iranian director was abroad, the Revolutionary Court in Tehran has upheld a one-year jail sentence.

items.title: High-functioning depression: When success masks suffering
items.content_html: People with depression don't always feel overwhelmed or struggle to get things done — some are highly efficient and productive, despite their low moods. So it can't be that bad, right? Wrong!

items.title: Why businesses are offering 'Quiet Hour'
items.content_html: More peace and quiet, less stress — the Quiet Hour is an initiative for people with invisible disabilities. An increasing number of stores and institutions in Germany are launching similar campaigns.

items.title: Desalination hopes and reformed poachers
items.content_html: EVs surge in Ethiopia, former poachers offer seahorse tours and India's ghost villages are repopulated.

items.title: SSRI antidepressants are often 'carelessly prescribed'
items.content_html: SSRIs, like Prozac and Zoloft, are among the world's most-prescribed psychiatric drugs. These antidepressants help a lot of people. But they don't always work. In fact, some experts suggest they are overprescribed.

items.title: Why Yad Vashem is coming to Germany
items.content_html: "Remember" — every child in Israel knows the meaning of this Jewish commandment: Never forget the Holocaust. Now Yad Vashem wants to make sure people in Germany remember it too.

items.title: Germans at Russia's SPIEF: Who's really there?
items.content_html: Russian officials and media claim German businesses are comming back to the St. Petersburg Economic Forum despite the war in Ukraine. But who is actually attending Russia's flagship event this year?

items.title: Can Hawaii wean itself off oil imports?
items.content_html: Hawaii imports much of its fuel — and pays the price. From solar to geothermal, the state is searching for a way out of fossil fuel dependence.

items.title: Iran war: Even a peace deal won't fix energy crunch
items.content_html: The growing energy and supply chain crisis is unlikely to ease, even if the US and Iran step back from the brink. Experts warn that reopening the Strait of Hormuz and repairing infrastructure could take months or years.

items.title: Google mosquito army: Scientists say 'we must take action'
items.content_html: Google's Debug research program plans to release millions of sterile mosquitoes to fight species that spread diseases like dengue. How does the method work — and should humans interfere with nature like this?

items.title: Saudi Arabia and Russia draw closer amid OPEC upheaval
items.content_html: As Saudi Arabia and Russia deepen cooperation, their combined influence over global oil supply is growing. Could this shift the balance of power in energy markets?

items.title: Who owns the billions in Germany's dormant bank accounts?
items.content_html: Billions of euros sit untouched in dormant bank accounts in Germany. With heirs unaware and no central register in place, banks and politicians are debating who should ultimately control the money.
